reasoning: Based on the pattern where Latin final '-o' corresponds to Umbrian final '-u' (e.g., 'ferto' -> 'fertu', 'canito' -> 'kanetu', 'sisto' -> 'sestu'). Applying this to 'ito' yields 'itu'.

Latin 'i' often becomes Umbrian 'e' (e.g., 'istum' -> 'estu', 'tibi' -> 'tefe'). Latin final '-um' usually drops 'm' to '-u' (e.g., 'istum' -> 'estu', 'catulum' -> 'katlu'). The Latin consonant cluster 'ct' corresponds to Umbrian 'ht' (e.g., 'Sanctam' -> 'Sahta', 'auctor' -> 'uhtur' where 'auct' becomes 'uht'). Combining these, 'frictum' (fri-ct-um) becomes 'frehtu'.

The pattern for 'pro X' in Latin is 'Xper' in Umbrian (e.g., 'pro Petronia' -> 'Petruniaper', 'pro populo' -> 'pupluper'). The Latin 'e' in 're' seems to remain 'e' in Umbrian, as seen in 'ostento' (Latin) -> 'ustetu' (Umbrian) where a medial 'e' is retained. Thus, 're' -> 're', and 'pro re' becomes 'reper'.

Latin final '-s' often corresponds to Umbrian '-f' (e.g., 'ovis' -> 'uvef', 'tris' -> 'tref'). Latin 'o' often becomes Umbrian 'u' (e.g., 'ovis' -> 'uvef', 'ferto' -> 'fertu'). The Latin diphthong 'au' corresponds to Umbrian 'uh' (e.g., 'auctor' -> 'uhtur'). Applying these rules to 'tauros' yields 'tuhruf'.

Latin final '-um' usually drops 'm' to become '-u' (e.g., 'istum' -> 'estu', 'catulum' -> 'katlu'). Latin 'o' consistently becomes Umbrian 'u' (e.g., 'sisto' -> 'sestu', 'ferto' -> 'fertu', 'populo' -> 'puplu'). Applying these rules to 'forum' yields 'furu'.

Latin 'i' often becomes Umbrian 'e' (e.g., 'sisto' -> 'sestu', 'canito' -> 'kanetu', 'tibi' -> 'tefe'). Latin final '-o' consistently becomes Umbrian '-u' (e.g., 'sisto' -> 'sestu', 'ferto' -> 'fertu', 'canito' -> 'kanetu'). Applying these to 'inito' yields 'inetu'.
